symbols: lattice window — the barrier between Michal’s pride and David’s worship
pointing gesture — accusation and contempt directed at David’s dancing
crown or diadem on Michal — her royal status as Saul’s daughter
attendant women — witnesses to the domestic conflict between king and queen
description: An interior scene viewed through a lattice window. A richly dressed woman wearing a crown or diadem leans forward with a scornful expression, pointing outward through the window with one hand while gripping the lattice with the other. Behind her, three women in veils and head coverings look on with varying expressions—one appears shocked, another curious. The scene is warmly lit from the window opening. The woman’s posture conveys anger and contempt.
theological_description: This scene depicts the moment described in 2 Samuel 6:16, when Michal, daughter of Saul and wife of David, ‘looked out of the window and saw King David leaping and dancing before the Lord, and she despised him in her heart.’ David had been bringing the Ark of the Covenant into Jerusalem with uninhibited worship, dancing before the Lord with all his might (2 Samuel 6:14). Michal’s contempt reveals a fundamental spiritual divide: she prioritized royal dignity over wholehearted worship of God. Her rebuke of David—‘How the king of Israel honored himself today, uncovering himself before the eyes of his servants’ maids’ (2 Samuel 6:20)—was met with David’s declaration that he would become even more undignified for the Lord’s sake. The consequence was severe: Michal had no children to the day of her death (2 Samuel 6:23). This episode teaches that pride and concern for human opinion are incompatible with genuine worship, and that God honors the humble worshiper over the proud skeptic.

scripture_refs.reason: Michal looking through the window and despising David as he danced before the Lord
Michal’s rebuke of David and the consequence of her barrenness
David dancing before the Lord with all his might, the act Michal despises
Praise the Lord with dancing—the worship David practiced and Michal rejected
